perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” means all oral and written information furnished by, or on behalf of, PHCS to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below), which is not ge nerally known to the public, including, without limitation, all trade secrets, products, procedures, manuals, guidelines, reports, communic ations, information regarding names and requirements of current and prospective suppliers, vendors and customers, tec hnical information regarding current and prospective products and programs and the development and/or acquisition of future products, programs a nd enhancements, discoveries, concepts and ideas, nature and results of research and development activities, tec hnical information on product or program performance and reliability, processes, formulae, techniques, “know -how,” specifications, any “protected health information” as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, as amended from time to time , and any other material or information related to the business or activities of PHCS, whether or not protectible by patent, copyright or other laws, whe ther furnished before or after the date of this Agreement, together with any additional information der ived from such information and contained in all analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives, as well as al l copies and other reproductions thereof, whether in writing or stored or maintaine d in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Confidential Information” means all oral and written information furnished by, or on behalf of, PHCS to the Recipient or its Representatives (as defined below), which is not generally known to the public, including, without limitation, all trade secrets, products, procedures, manuals, guidelines, reports, communic ations, information regarding names and requirements of current and prospective suppliers, vendors and customers, tec hnical information regarding current and prospective products and programs and the development and/or acquisition of future products, programs a nd enhancements, discoveries, concepts and ideas, nature and results of research and development activities, tec hnical information on product or program performance and reliability, processes, formulae, techniques, “know -how,” specifications, any health information, as amended from time to time , and any other material or information related to the business or activities of PHCS, whether or not protectible by patent, copyright or other laws, whe ther furnished before or after the date of this Agreement, together with any additional information der ived from such information and contained in all analyses, compilations, studies or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives, as well as al l copies and other reproductions thereof, whether in writing or stored or maintaine d in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific reference to "protected health information” as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, and replacing it with the broader term "health information" it changes the scope of what constitutes confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of confidential health information beyond the scope defined in HIPAA, potentially leading to confusion and non-compliance with HIPAA regulations. HIPAA specifically defines 'protected health information' (PHI), and using the term 'health information' creates ambiguity about what data the agreement protects.

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock A locked padlock ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Navigate to: The HIPAA Privacy Rule The HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes national standards to protect individuals' medical records and other individually identifiable health information (collectively defined as “protected health information”) and applies to health plans, health care clearinghouses, and those health care providers that conduct certain health care transactions electronically. The Rule requires appropriate safeguards to protect the privacy of protected health information and sets limits and conditions on the uses and disclosures that may be made of such information without an individual’s authorization. The Rule also gives individuals rights over their protected health information, including rights to examine and obtain a copy of their health records, to direct a covered entity to transmit to a third party an electronic copy of their protected health information in an electronic health record, and to request corrections. The Privacy Rule is located at 45 CFR Part 160 and Subparts A and E of Part 164 . Click here to view the combined regulation text of all HIPAA Administrative Simplification Regulations found at 45 CFR 160, 162, and 164. Privacy Rule History April 26, 2024 - HIPAA Privacy Rule to Support Reproductive Health Care Privacy - Final Rule April 17, 2023 - HIPAA Privacy Rule to Support Reproductive Health Care Privacy - Proposed Rule March 10, 2021 - Extension of Comment Period for Modifications to the HIPAA Privacy Rule to Support, and Remove Barriers to, Coordinated Care and Individual Engagement – Proposed Rule January 21, 2021 - Modifications to the HIPAA Privacy Rule to Empower Patients, Improve Coordinated Care, and Reduce Regulatory Burdens - Proposed Rule December 14, 2018 - Modifying the HIPAA Rules to Improve Coordinated Care - Request for Information January 6, 2016 - HIPAA Privacy Rule and the National Instant Criminal Background Check System (NICS) - Final Rule February 6, 2014 - Patients' Access to Test Reports Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule and the Clinical Laboratory Improvement Amendments of 1988 (CLIA) Program - Final Rule January 7, 2014 - HIPAA Privacy Rule and NICS - Proposed Rule April 23, 2013 - HIPAA Privacy Rule and NICS - Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking January 25, 2013 - Modifications to the HIPAA Privacy, Security, Enforcement, and Breach Notification Rules under the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health (HITECH) Act and the Genetic Information Nondiscrimination Act, and Other Modifications - Final Rule (The "Omnibus HIPAA Final Rule") September 14, 2011 - Patients' Access to Test Reports Under the HIPAA Privacy Rule and CLIA Program - Proposed Rule May 31, 2011 - HIPAA Privacy Rule Accounting of Disclosures Under the HITECH Act - Proposed Rule July 14, 2010 - Modifications to the HIPAA Privacy, Security, and Enforcement Rules under the HITECH Act - Proposed Rule May 3, 2010 - HIPAA Privacy Rule Accounting of Disclosures Under the HITECH Act - Reques
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifically references "protected health information" as defined in HIPAA regulations, 45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164, thereby aligning with the legally defined scope of PHI. The changed text replaces this with "health information," which is a much broader term not specifically defined by HIPAA, as stated in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from HHS.gov explicitly states that the HIPAA Privacy Rule establishes national standards to protect "protected health information" and defines it as individually identifiable health information. By broadening the term, the modified contract introduces ambiguity and potentially weakens the protection afforded to PHI under HIPAA, creating a misaligned terminology legal contradiction. This undermines the specific legal standard established by HIPAA.

perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Representatives” means, with respect to PHCS or the Recipient, their directors , officers, shareholders, members, managers, partners, employees, affiliat es and controlling persons, and their agents, advisors and representatives (including without limitation attorneys, accountants and financial advisors).
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, the term “Representatives” means, with respect to PHCS or the Recipient, their directors , officers, shareholders, members, managers, partners, employees, affiliat es and controlling persons, and their agents, advisors and representatives (including only attorneys and accountants).
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of 'Representatives' to only attorneys and accountants, the contract excludes other advisors like financial advisors, thus reducing the scope of liability for unauthorized disclosures.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the generally accepted understanding of who can be considered as acting on behalf of a corporation including reliance on other reports. Advisors could be considered to be financial advisors, or other members.

(here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New Jersey Revised Statutes Title 14A - Corporations, General Section 14A:6-14 - Liability of directors; reliance on records and reports Universal Citation: NJ Rev Stat § 14A:6-14 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 14A:6-14. Liability of directors; reliance on records and reports Liability of directors; reliance on records and reports. (1) Directors and members of any committee designated by the board shall discharge their duties in good faith and with that degree of diligence, care and skill which ordinarily prudent people would exercise under similar circumstances in like positions. (2) In discharging their duties, directors and members of any committee designated by the board shall not be liable if, acting in good faith, they rely (a) Upon the opinion of counsel for the corporation; (b) Upon written reports setting forth financial data concerning the corporation and prepared by an independent public accountant or certified public accountant or firm of such accountants; (c) Upon financial statements, books of account or reports of the corporation represented to them to be correct by the president, the officer of the corporation having charge of its books of account, or the person presiding at a meeting of the board; or (d) Upon written reports of committees of the board. (3) A director shall not be personally liable to the corporation or its shareholders for damages for breach of duty as a director if and to the extent that such liability has been eliminated or limited by a provision in the certificate of inc
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by limiting the definition of 'Representatives' to only attorneys and accountants, creates a legal contradiction. The original text includes a broader range of advisors. The Law Explanation states that the modified text contradicts the 'generally accepted understanding of who can be considered as acting on behalf of a corporation including reliance on other reports,' noting that advisors could be financial advisors, or other members. The provided snippet from New Jersey Revised Statutes Section 14A:6-14 (2022) discusses the 'Liability of directors; reliance on records and reports' and describes situations in which directors are not liable if they rely upon certain opinions and reports. Specifically, 14A:6-14 (2)(a) mentions reliance 'Upon the opinion of counsel for the corporation', and 14A:6-14 (2)(b) mentions reliance 'Upon written reports setting forth financial data concerning the corporation and prepared by an independent public accountant or certified public accountant or firm of such accountants'. By narrowing the definition of 'Representatives', the modified contract text reduces the scope of reliance and therefore may expose directors to liability if they rely on other advisors, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither PHCS nor its Representatives make any representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. Each Recipient agree s that neither PHCS nor any of its Representatives shall have any liability to the Recipient or its Representatives resulting from the use of the Confidential Information supplied by PHCS or any of its Repres entatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither PHCS nor its Representatives make any representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information, unless such information is intentionally misleading. Each Recipient agree s that neither PHCS nor any of its Representatives shall have any liability to the Recipient or its Representatives resulting from the use of the Confidential Information supplied by PHCS or any of its Repres entatives.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the clause "unless such information is intentionally misleading" introduces ambiguity about liability and contradicts the intent of the original clause, which aimed for a complete disclaimer of liability. It creates a loophole.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the original text attempts to limit liability, the added exception might create a legal argument that PHCS is liable for negligent misrepresentation, particularly if the information was provided with knowledge of its potential inaccuracy or incompleteness, thus contradicting the original no-liability clause. Liability for negligent misrepresentation, depending on the jurisdiction, may be imposed even without intent to mislead.

common law provinces pertaining to contractual and pre-contractual negotiation v t e In common law jurisdictions, a misrepresentation is a false or misleading [ 1 ] statement of fact made during negotiations by one party to another, the statement then inducing that other party to enter into a contract. [ 2 ] [ 3 ] The misled party may normally rescind the contract, and sometimes may be awarded damages as well (or instead of rescission). The law of misrepresentation is an amalgam of contract and tort; and its sources are common law, equity and statute. In England and Wales, the common law was amended by the Misrepresentation Act 1967. The general principle of misrepresentation has been adopted by the United States and other former British colonies, e.g. India. Representation and contract terms [ edit ] A "representation" is a pre-contractual statement made during negotiations. [ 4 ] If a representation has been incorporated into the contract as a term , [ 5 ] then the normal remedies for breach of contract apply. Factors that determine whether or not a representation has become a term include: The relative expertise of the parties. [ 6 ] [ 7 ] The reliance that one party has shown on the statement. [ 8 ] The reassuran
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the disclaimer of liability. The original text disclaims all liability but the addition of "unless such information is intentionally misleading" opens the door for liability based on misrepresentation. The Law Explanation indicates a potential liability for negligent misrepresentation even without intent. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines misrepresentation as a false or misleading statement that induces a party to enter a contract, and that the misled party may normally rescind the contract, and sometimes may be awarded damages as well. This contradicts the original clause's intent to have no liability, therefore a legal contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed (such destruction to be certified in writing) by an authorized officer and not retained in any form or for any reason.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon PHCS’ request, all Confidential Information (and all copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part thereof), whether in writing or stored or maintained in or by electronic, magnetic or other means, media or devices, sh all be returned or destroyed (such destruction to be certified in writing) by an authorized officer and not retained in any form or for any reason, unless retention is required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless retention is required by law" creates an exception that might be interpreted broadly, potentially undermining the obligation to return or destroy confidential information. Some laws may require retaining some data, thus it is important to be specific.
perturbation.law_explanation: This introduces legal uncertainty. For example, compliance with the Consumer Fraud Act could mandate retaining specific sales records and documentation, potentially conflicting with the general mandate to destroy all Confidential Information at PHCS's request. The added clause now means that the company might inadvertently be in violation of a Consumer Fraud Act.

(a) The term "advertisement" shall include the attempt directly or indirectly by publication, dissemination, solicitation, indorsement or circulation or in any other way to induce directly or indirectly any person to enter or not enter into any obligation or acquire any title or interest in any merchandise or to increase the consumption thereof or to make any loan; (b) The term "Attorney General" shall mean the Attorney General of the State of New Jersey or any person acting on his behalf; (c) The term "merchandise" shall include any objects, wares, goods, commodities, services or anything offered, directly or indirectly to the public for sale; (d) The term "person" as used in this act shall include any natural person or his legal representative, partnership, corporation, company, trust, business entity or association, and any agent, employee, salesman, partner, officer, director, member, stockholder, associate, trustee or cestuis que trustent thereof; (e) The term "sale" shall include any sale, rental or distribution, offer for sale, rental or distribution or attempt directly or indirectly to sell, rent or distribute; (f) The term "senior citizen" means a natural person 60 years of age or older. L.1960,c.39,s.1; amended 1967, c.301, s.1; 1999, c.298, s.2. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. New
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception ('unless retention is required by law') to the original, which mandated the destruction of confidential information upon request. This exception, while seemingly reasonable, creates a potential conflict. The Law Explanation suggests compliance with the Consumer Fraud Act may require retaining certain sales records, which would then contradict the original agreement to destroy all confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2, defines "advertisement" and "merchandise" which, while not directly stating a retention requirement, implies business records exist and are subject to legal definitions. The altered text creates an ambiguity where complying with one legal requirement (retention under consumer fraud laws, potentially) violates another (destruction of confidential information as per the original contract), therefore undermining the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of New Jersey without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and co nstrued in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without giving effect to the conflict of laws provisions thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from New Jersey to Delaware can have significant legal implications, as Delaware law is often more favorable to corporations but may not provide the same protections in confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: By switching to Delaware law, even if the parties or the agreement have no substantial relationship to Delaware, it may be challenged as a violation of choice-of-law principles, particularly if it seeks to evade New Jersey law that would otherwise apply and protect the interests of one party more strongly. Also, New Jersey has a strong interest in this case, since that's the location of PHCS.

perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from New Jersey to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that switching to Delaware law, especially without a substantial relationship to Delaware, can be challenged as a violation of choice-of-law principles, especially if done to evade New Jersey law that would otherwise apply. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that Delaware law may be more favorable to corporations but not provide the same protections in confidentiality agreements. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address choice-of-law principles, the Perturbation explanation in combination with the law explanation indicate that this change introduces a legal contradiction, because changing the governing law can be viewed as an attempt to evade the protections afforded under New Jersey Law.
